text.title: Build Or Waitlist Decision Tree Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Build Or Waitlist Decision Tree application MUST enforce, plus a terminal skip-this-methodology rule.

rule.statement: Build OR waitlist only if ≥3 distinct customer requests exist (named customer + verbatim quote). Below the floor → decline with anti-roadmap entry.
rule.rationale: Demand-evidence research — anchors Build Or Waitlist Decision Tree rule `three-request-floor`.

rule.statement: Before promising a build, founder MUST verify current sprint capacity has ≥4 free hours for the work. Promise without capacity = broken commitment.
rule.rationale: Capacity-management discipline — anchors Build Or Waitlist Decision Tree rule `capacity-check-pre-build`.

rule.statement: Build MUST advance a published roadmap outcome. Off-roadmap builds trigger a roadmap re-review, not silent inclusion.
rule.rationale: Roadmap-discipline practice — anchors Build Or Waitlist Decision Tree rule `roadmap-alignment`.

rule.statement: Every build decision MUST carry a kill-criterion (metric + threshold + observation window) BEFORE work starts. Builds without kill-criteria run forever.
rule.rationale: Falsifiability discipline — anchors Build Or Waitlist Decision Tree rule `kill-criterion-before-build`.

rule.statement: Every ask MUST produce a single decision-record (build / waitlist / decline) with rationale + revisit date. Verbal-only rulings are forbidden.
rule.rationale: Decision-record practice — anchors Build Or Waitlist Decision Tree rule `decision-record-required`.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition or triggered skip, then route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
